shorts_scripts.hook: Stop doing squats like this. Here's what it does to your spine.
beats: Look at your lower back during a rounded squat—the lumbar vertebrae pinch your spinal discs.
Now watch a neutral-spine squat: the load is distributed perfectly across your hips and glutes.
A rounded spine increases shear force on your discs by over two hundred percent.
shorts_scripts.payoff: Fixing your hip mobility instantly shifts the pressure from your spine to your quad fibers.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: A 3D translucent skeleton showing spinal discs glowing red and pinching under a rounded load, transitioning to green and perfectly aligned.

shorts_scripts.hook: What actually happens inside your body when you do pull-ups daily?
beats: Day 1 to 7: Your latissimus dorsi fibers micro-tear, sending satellite cells to repair them bigger.
Day 14: Your nervous system recruits dormant motor units in your forearms, exploding your grip strength.
Day 30: Your shoulder blades pull backward, correcting your rounded desk posture.
shorts_scripts.payoff: The daily hanging stretch decompresses your spine, temporarily making you up to one centimeter taller.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: A 3D camera zooming into back muscles to show cellular tearing, followed by the vertebrae stretching slightly apart.

shorts_scripts.hook: Running vs Jump Rope: Which destroys your knees faster?
beats: Every stride in running sends shockwaves three times your body weight up your shinbone.
But jump rope forces you to land on the balls of your feet, using your calves as natural springs.
This elastic recoil absorbs the impact before it ever reaches your knee cartilage.
shorts_scripts.payoff: Jump rope actually reduces peak knee joint stress by up to thirty percent compared to running.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: Side-by-side 3D skeletal view showing red shockwaves travelling up the runner's knee versus blue cushioned waves dissipating in the jumper's ankle.
